title: Written By Adachi Sakura
description: A story within a story. Adachi's mother—a novelist—struggles with her manuscript about two sisters on a dying planet. A meditation on writing, loss, and the impossible distance between people.
content: What I was doing just now? Working, obviously. Isn't it obvious? Even if I was on the phone. You think I sound like I just woke up?? No—you wouldn't even know what I sound like when I just woke up… probably. Yeah, it's fine, the manuscript is fine, really… No, I'm not rubbing my eyes, and I'm not sleepy. I definitely didn't yawn. So this call is about… the party I'm skipping? Mm-hmm… Ahh, right, that thing. I think a letter came about it… and the deadline's today? Ah, yeah. Am I going? Why would I? I don't know anyone, and buffets don't interest me. And if I go, it's not like I'll magically make friends. That's true. But even if I did make some, and then what? It's not like we'd work together. Anyway, I don't know how to make friends. I've never made any. Why? Even if I say I simply don't need friends, you'd just assume I'm gloomy, wouldn't you? No, I'm not being self-deprecating. I just… don't get along with people very well. You know that already? Well… yeah. Even if people from the editorial office invite me, I'm basically just décor to them. Talking to my editor—well, I'm used to it. I mean, I've already mastered the art of tossing out random excuses to delay deadlines. No, no, that's a joke. Not 'random.' I agonize every time over how to delay it. And waiting for the reply after sending the message is stressful too—like, is this okay? Are they mad? Anyway, what were we talking about? Ah, the party. It's completely different from the year-end party. I only ever went to one of those. Never again. Bright, lively places aren't for me. Honestly, maybe writing really is the only thing that suits me. Or maybe I'm just unsuited for anything else. At this point, no other job works for me. Writing is the only way I have left to survive. I don't even know if living is simple or difficult anymore. The work itself is understandable at least—that's something. Ah, I got off-topic without noticing. I'm just not used to talking. My ability to weave words is weak, and all those expressions I rely on in my novels—where do they go at times like this? Well anyway. Just say I'm not attending. Tell them I'm busy with the manuscript. And yes, the manuscript is fine. Really. Yes, yes, I know—you think I always say that. I think so too. Okay, okay, I'll do my best. Eh, an extra bonus story? Hah… ah, seriously? I'll manage something. It'll be fine. Thank you… yes, yes—— haaah— It had been a long time since I'd spoken that much. My brain was still foggy from having just woken up; I couldn't even remember half of what I said. I grabbed the glasses beside my pillow, crawled out of bed, and went to the bathroom. After rinsing my mouth, I splashed cold water on my face—only to realize afterward that I hadn't taken off my glasses. Too late. The lenses were dotted with water, and I silently removed them to wipe them clean. I'd only recently started wearing glasses, so the habit hadn't sunk in yet. With my face and glasses washed, I headed to my workroom. I turned on the heater first. Then I wake the dormant computer. And there it was—the half-finished manuscript staring back at me. 'Well then.' My fingers tapped the keyboard, lightly knocking as if on a door. I hadn't checked the time, and my work hours were irregular as always. ※ I still couldn't feel the reality of it—that I would never see my little sister again. Under a sky tinted purple and red, wind brushed against my cheeks. Past the edge of our empty, dying town, the plants were still growing wild. The sound of my footsteps through the grass belonged only to me now. Once, there had always been another pair of steps behind mine. Even now, I sometimes think I can hear them. But every time I turn around, nothing's there. Just empty space beside me. There was no need to hurry home, vegetables in hand. Letting time waste away didn't matter anymore. As long as I managed to live on my own, losing time—losing hours—was… harmless enough. The thought didn't match my life at all, and a dull laugh slipped out. My time was far shorter than it used to be. The streets were deserted—easy to walk on. Cars that once passed nonstop were gone too, leaving the roads wide and bare. Walking straight down the center felt like being the last person alive. But if I listened closely, I could still feel traces of other people somewhere in the distance—faint, but undeniably there. That thought alone kept my eyes drifting from side to side as I walked. I walked past the town's only hospital—if it could still be called one. A slow trickle of people drifted in and out, nothing like what it used to be. Once, the place had been impressive: sprawling grounds, a full staff, every piece of equipment it needed. Now, you could probably fit everything that still functioned onto the first floor. I wondered what it felt like to be a doctor in a time like this. Whether treatment succeeded or failed hardly mattered. Either way, death was coming. Even a doctor couldn't extend the life of a dying star. I lifted my gaze. The sky was wrapped in something uncertain, the cycles broken. This planet—this star—was approaching its end. The star itself would disappear. The signs of its collapse were already written overhead. The long stretch of abnormal weather had calmed lately, but that only made it feel like the last quiet breath before the fall. Soon the environment would turn harsh again, and my life might end before the star itself did. 'I'm home.' I knew no one would answer, but the words slipped out anyway. Nothing felt real yet. The lingering warmth of someone who used to stand beside me hadn't faded. I set the vegetables in the sink and breathed in. In a house without family, every sound felt thin. If I held my breath, it felt like I might vanish into the walls. The tap had long stopped working. Fetching water was one of my daily chores now. Even after civilization and technology crumbled, humans still couldn't live alone. I put everything away and sat down in the hallway. Cleaning the house every day didn't mean anything anymore, but I still did it. Whether I was moving or standing still, my mind drifted endlessly. Maybe that meant I was already half-dead. I inhaled the air of the house. A faint, familiar scent still lingered, soothing me. But with no one else here, what exactly was I smelling? After our parents died, it had just been me and my little sister. Now she is gone too. She went far, far away—so far I had no idea when she'd reach her destination. My sister had left this star, choosing to migrate to another planet before this one collapsed. My brilliant sister even secured a spot for me. Then she came home to tell me. I remember it clearly—she hardly ever got angry. 'Come with me, Sis! Why would you stay here!?' It might have been the first time she had ever shouted seriously. And yes—I refused. 'There's… Mom and Dad's graves.' 'Graves don't mean anything. The dead aren't in them. When you die, that's it. There's nowhere else to go.' She was completely right. I understood that. But my feet wouldn't move. I couldn't leave this star. She reached for my hand to drag me along, but when our eyes met, she stopped. She wanted me to come, but… 'What's the point of dying in a place like this…?' 'Well, even if you go to some far-off planet, you'll die eventually.' 'Don't talk like that.' 'That's just how it feels to me.' Dying here or dying out there—it didn't feel different. And the migration program had limited seats, uncertain outcomes. Better to give the chance to someone who truly wanted it. 'Besides, the planet they're migrating to already has life. If humans push it aside and reshape the environment… the same thing will happen again someday.' Endlessly migrating could be a way to live, I suppose. But it didn't appeal to me. So I told her I wouldn't go. Her face flushed with anger, and when she spoke again, her voice turned sharp—like a blade dragging low across the floor. 'In the end, Sis… you just can't let go, can you? You're clinging to someone who's already gone.' Her scorn slid between my ribs like a buried knife. After that, every time we met, she asked me again to board the ship. And every time, I apologized—and refused. I truly didn't want to leave. But when I said that, she thought I was making fun of her. Her anger only deepened. I didn't know what to do, so I looked away. Even though we had so little time left. Even though we were about to part forever. We wasted it on arguments and silence. On the day of departure, she didn't speak until the very end. She sat in the entryway with her luggage, perfectly still. I had nothing to say either, so I watched her back, memorizing it before it disappeared forever. We had been together since the day she was born. And now she was about to disappear from my life. We would never see each other again. A distance that had never existed opened between us. To her, I would soon be as good as dead. And to me, she was about to die as well. Nothing came to mind to say. As the moment drew closer, I sorted through every possible word and discarded all of them. 'It's strange to say this but… take care, okay?' I couldn't bring myself to say goodbye. All I managed was a vague, clumsy wish for her well-being. She stood without a word, put on her shoes, and opened the door. 'Why… didn't you ask me to stay?' Those were the last words she ever said in this house. Left behind, I stared through the open doorway at the blue sky. 'I wanted to.' I really did. '…Ah—' When you're alone, memories multiply without end. If I had asked her to stay, maybe she would've stayed by my side. But I couldn't drag someone who had chosen life into dying with me. And I knew—without a single doubt—that if she died, I would mourn her. Forcing her to feel the same pain felt unforgivable. Of course I imagined living with her in that new world. But even there, she would have been out of reach. Just like now—no matter how far I stretch out my hand, I can't touch her. She died long before the star did, on this very earth, leaving me behind. She went somewhere farther than any star, somewhere no one can follow. Looking back, the whispers about the star's dwindling life had already begun. Then came the sudden collapse, the hastening end. Even if we stayed still, the end kept marching toward us. I once joked that maybe the star mirrored my sorrow—that it chose its fate because of me. But the only thing that truly ended with her death was my heart. Sitting too long only made me dwell on the past, so I stood and went to the kitchen. With the power failing, I had to preserve food the old-fashioned way. My parents had loved those methods; they raised me to follow them too. My sister had disliked those outdated habits—she was modern, and that's why she could fly to space. As for me, washing vegetables in cold water felt natural. Not everyone who stayed on this star did so by choice. After the migration plan was announced, the world fell into chaos. Those weren't good times. Even so, the people who remained now faced the quieting world head-on. Lonely, yes—but maybe just right. Once my chores were finished, I flopped down on the hallway floor like a starfish. The impact rattled my bones unpleasantly. 'You've gotten thinner,' a dry voice echoed in my mind. Eyes closed, I heard it: two sets of footsteps—mine and my sister's. Ghosts trailing after me. Sometimes I almost felt like she was right there. Yesterday I even hallucinated a conversation with her. Her voice in the illusion was frighteningly accurate. The next day, after finishing work in the fields, I went to pick flowers. For my family's graves—and for hers. Until the sky crushes us between cosmic distortions, my feelings for her won't change. My sister once said there's nothing after death—and I believe her. Because there is no afterlife, I can give everything to the living. And yet, just as she lingers in my world like a fading hallucination, every street, every sign, every seasonal flower drags her memory back to me, as if the universe itself refuses to let her go. Time spent with her flows through me like sand. Whenever I touch it, it feels like she's still alive. If I abandoned everything and left for a distant star, nothing would remain to bring her back into my heart. This home—this dying star—is closer to her than any other world. My sister saw through all my selfish reasons. 'As expected of my little sister.' That cold, proud thought warmed into something soft. Soft enough to make me sleepy. ※ '…What a pain…' I'd hoped ideas would surface as I wrote, but instead I ran straight into a wall. The sister—that was the problem. What should I do? I sat up straight, even dragging out the sound of my sigh. The younger sister could do anything in the new world. The older sister, however, had nowhere left to grow. She had no one important, no one close. Telling the story from both of their perspectives and letting them meet—that was the classic structure. But where should it end? Maybe it would be simpler to let the older sister's story close neatly… I even considered turning it into a short story. Chin in hand, I stared at the screen. When I first pictured this story, I was certain it would work. I thought it had that feeling. But once I actually started writing, there was nothing. Nothing at all. This happened often. Stories like this were rarely well received, while works that felt merely average sometimes sold astonishingly well. Even when people praised me as an author, it only made one thing clear—my own instincts didn't seem to amount to much. I took off my glasses and rubbed my eyes. Leaving the chair, I walked toward the balcony. The moment I opened the window, cold air poured over me like a river. A small sound slipped out of my throat. I'd planned to use my drowsiness as an excuse to stop working, but the sudden chill snapped me awake instead. On the tiny balcony, I brushed off the chair and sat down. Colder than I had expected. I wrapped my arms around myself, shivering under the night sky. A faint white breath rose from my mouth. I exhaled a bigger puff and watched it vanish. I rolled my stiff shoulders and sat up straighter. Winter, lit only by pale light spilling from a single window, bore its usual sharpness. I haven't been outside much lately. Work kept me wrapped in warmth, almost letting me forget the season entirely. Since graduating high school, I haven't gone anywhere. I lived off my writing alone. A novel I submitted had been accepted and, unexpectedly, did well. If there's any upside to this job, it's the lack of social contact. Aside from the occasional phone call with my editor, my world existed entirely on the screen in front of me. Even so, I've written about all kinds of things—none of them drawn from my own life. Imagination fills in what I've never done, what I've never felt. I have no friends. No one I love. No sister, either. And yet I'm supposed to write about what it means to be an older sister. If someone told me, The sky is about to fall—run, I'd probably board the ship without hesitation. I don't feel attached to this world. If my deadline disappeared tomorrow, I doubt I'd care. It's strange. Before this job, time felt endless. Now it slips through my fingers, and I'm always chasing it. Still—realism in fiction isn't reality. Sisters like these might exist somewhere in the universe. If even one reader can feel that, perhaps I've done well as a novelist. Sending my writing out into the world feels like sending letters between stars. These books—messages—travel vast distances, hoping to reach someone. …Ah. If that's the case, maybe the older sister could write a letter to the younger one. That could work. A new direction. Maybe freezing out here wasn't a waste after all. I pushed back from the chair and stood, knees stiff with cold. Leaning on the balcony rail, winter wrapped around me. Like untouched snow against my skin, the cold drifted in fragments on the wind. Haa—I inhaled deeply, the cold flooding from tongue to throat. I looked up—no stars in the heavy sky. As if searching for one, my hand drifted through the darkness. When will I ever feel something like that—something stronger than love, stronger than life itself? A feeling powerful enough to make me abandon what's close to me, to face death tomorrow and still reach for it. Will I ever reach that point? Will I ever understand what this book might mean to someone? '…Don't expect too much from a manuscript that isn't even finished.' My self-mockery rose with my breath, fading into the cold air. And in the direction it disappeared, I thought I saw a tiny star.
